section.title: Josiah's Reforms and the Book of the Law
section.sectionRationale: While still a young man, Josiah begins seeking the God of David, then systematically purges Judah and even former Israelite territory of idolatry years before any book of the law is found. During temple repairs, Hilkiah discovers the long lost Book of the Law itself, and when it is read aloud, Josiah tears his clothes in grief, sends to inquire of the LORD, and receives Huldah's prophecy that judgment is fixed but delayed because of his own tender, humbled heart, leading him to renew the covenant before the whole assembly.
section.thesis: Josiah's reform began with seeking God before he had the book, and only deepened into covenant renewal once the book was found and heard, showing that a heart already turned toward God recognizes and receives His word rightly the moment it is rediscovered, rather than needing the word to produce the first spark of devotion.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Josiah's early seeking of God and his purge of idolatry, verses 1 to 7; the temple repairs and the discovery of the Book of the Law, verses 8 to 18; Josiah's grief and Huldah's prophecy, verses 19 to 28; and the public covenant renewal, verses 29 to 33.

units.label: Josiah destroys idolatry
units.note: he began to seek the God of his father David

verses.text: Josiah was eight years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem thirty-one years.
And he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD and walked in the ways of his father David; he did not turn aside to the right or to the left.

In the eighth year of his reign, while he was still young, Josiah began to {{darash|seek}} the God of his father David, and in the twelfth year he began to cleanse Judah and Jerusalem of the high places, the Asherah poles, the carved idols, and the cast images.

Then in his presence the altars of the Baals were torn down, and he cut to pieces the incense altars that were above them. He shattered the Asherah poles, the carved idols, and the cast images, crushed them to dust, and scattered them over the graves of those who had sacrificed to them.
Then he burned the bones of the priests on their altars. So he cleansed Judah and Jerusalem.
Josiah did the same in the cities of Manasseh, Ephraim, and Simeon, as far as Naphtali, and in the ruins around them.
He tore down the altars and Asherah poles, crushed the idols to powder, and cut to pieces all the incense altars throughout the land of Israel. Then he returned to Jerusalem.

units.label: Hilkiah finds the Book of the Law
units.note: I have found the Book of the Law

verses.text: Now in the eighteenth year of his reign, in order to cleanse the land and the temple, Josiah sent Shaphan son of Azaliah, Maaseiah the governor of the city, and Joah son of Joahaz, the recorder, to repair the house of the LORD his God.

So they went to Hilkiah the high priest and gave him the money that had been brought into the house of God, which the Levites who guarded the doors had collected from the people of Manasseh and Ephraim, from all the remnant of Israel, from all Judah and Benjamin, and from the people of Jerusalem.
They put it into the hands of those supervising the work in the house of the LORD, who in turn gave it to the workmen restoring and repairing the house of the LORD.
They also gave money to the carpenters and builders to buy dressed stone, as well as timbers for couplings and beams for the buildings that the kings of Judah had allowed to deteriorate.

And the men did the work faithfully. The Levites overseeing them were Jahath and Obadiah, descendants of Merari, and Zechariah and Meshullam, descendants of Kohath. Other Levites, all skilled with musical instruments,
were over the laborers and supervised all who did the work, task by task. Some of the Levites were secretaries, officers, and gatekeepers.
While they were bringing out the money that had been taken into the house of the LORD, Hilkiah the priest found the Book of the Law of the LORD given by Moses.
And Hilkiah said to Shaphan the scribe, "I have found the Book of the Law in the house of the LORD!" And he gave it to Shaphan.
Then Shaphan brought the book to the king and reported, "Your servants are doing all that has been placed in their hands.
They have paid out the money that was found in the house of the LORD and have put it into the hands of the supervisors and workers."
Moreover, Shaphan the scribe told the king, "Hilkiah the priest has given me a book." And Shaphan read it in the presence of the king.
When the king heard the words of the Law, he {{qara|tore}} his clothes
and commanded Hilkiah, Ahikam son of Shaphan, Abdon son of Micah, Shaphan the scribe, and Asaiah the servant of the king:

"Go and inquire of the LORD for me and for those remaining in Israel and Judah concerning the words in the book that has been found. For great is the wrath of the LORD that has been poured out on us because our fathers have not kept the word of the LORD by doing all that is written in this book."

So Hilkiah and those the king had designated went and spoke to Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum son of Tokhath, the son of Hasrah, the keeper of the wardrobe. She lived in Jerusalem, in the Second District.
And Huldah said to them, "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'Tell the man who sent you
that this is what the LORD says: I am about to bring calamity on this place and on its people, according to all the curses written in the book that has been read in the presence of the king of Judah,

because they have forsaken Me and burned incense to other gods, that they might provoke Me to anger with all the works of their hands. My wrath will be poured out upon this place and will not be quenched.'
But as for the king of Judah, who sent you to inquire of the LORD, tell him that this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'As for the words that you heard,

because your heart was {{rakak|tender}} and you humbled yourself before God when you heard His words against this place and against its people, and because you have humbled yourself before Me and have torn your clothes and wept before Me, I have heard you,' declares the LORD.

'Now I will indeed gather you to your fathers, and you will be gathered to your grave in peace. Your eyes will not see all the calamity that I will bring on this place and on its people.'" So they brought her answer back to the king.

units.label: Josiah renews the covenant
units.note: all the people carried out the covenant

verses.text: Then the king summoned all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.

And he went up to the house of the LORD with all the people of Judah and Jerusalem, as well as the priests and the Levites, all the people great and small, and in their hearing he read all the words of the Book of the Covenant that had been found in the house of the LORD.

So the king stood by the pillar and made a covenant before the LORD to follow the LORD and to keep His commandments, decrees, and statutes with all his heart and all his soul, and to carry out the words of the covenant that were written in this book.
Then he had everyone in Jerusalem and Benjamin take a stand in agreement to it. So all the people of Jerusalem carried out the covenant of God, the God of their fathers.

And Josiah removed all the abominations from all the lands belonging to the Israelites, and he required everyone in Israel to serve the LORD their God. Throughout his reign they did not turn aside from following the LORD, the God of their fathers.

darash.gloss: To seek, inquire, or resort to. Josiah began to darash, seek, the God of David at just sixteen years old, four years before he began actively cleansing the land and six years before the Book of the Law was even found, showing that his devotion preceded and did not depend on rediscovering that written word.

qara.gloss: To tear or rend. Josiah qara, tears, his clothes the moment he hears the Law's words read aloud, the traditional expression of grief and alarm; his immediate, visible response models the right reaction to encountering God's word after generations of neglect.

rakak.gloss: To be tender or soft. The LORD commends Josiah specifically because his heart was rakak, tender, when he heard the Law's warnings, in contrast to the stubborn, hardened hearts of prior generations; a tender heart, not a flawless nation, is what secures Josiah's own personal peace despite the coming judgment.

basic.context: Josiah becomes king at eight years old, and remarkably, by sixteen he has already begun seeking the God of David on his own initiative, years before any lost scroll of the Law is ever mentioned. By twenty, he begins an extensive purge of idolatry that reaches from Jerusalem across the former northern kingdom's territory. Only later, during temple repairs in his eighteenth year, does Hilkiah the priest discover the actual Book of the Law itself, apparently lost or neglected for generations. When it is read to Josiah, he immediately tears his clothes in grief and sends to inquire of the LORD through the prophetess Huldah, who confirms that judgment is fixed against Judah for generations of unfaithfulness, but promises Josiah personally that because his heart was tender and he humbled himself, he will be spared from seeing that calamity in his own lifetime. Josiah then leads the entire nation in a public covenant renewal, reading the Book of the Covenant aloud and having everyone commit to it.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that carefully separates Josiah's personal devotion, which begins years before the book's discovery, from the book's later confirming and intensifying effect on both his reform and the nation's covenant commitment. It is not suggesting God's word is unnecessary for genuine devotion, since its rediscovery clearly deepens and formalizes everything that follows, but it does show that a heart already inclined toward God recognizes and receives that word rightly the moment it is heard.
basic.originalLanguages: Josiah begins to darash, seek, God years before any book is found, showing personal devotion preceding written confirmation. When that Book of the Law is finally read to him, he immediately qara, tears, his clothes in grief, the traditional sign of mourning and alarm. Huldah's prophecy commends him specifically because his heart was rakak, tender, softened rather than hardened by what he heard, the very quality that secures God's personal mercy toward him despite the nation's fixed judgment.
basic.typology: The long lost Book of the Law, rediscovered during temple repairs and immediately recognized as authoritative and urgent the moment it is heard, previews the pattern of God's word being neglected and then powerfully rediscovered throughout redemptive history, culminating in the reformation principle that Scripture, once truly heard, always demands response, repentance, and renewed covenant.
basic.christ: Josiah's tender heart, humbled the moment he heard God's word even before any personal wrongdoing of his own was in view, models the posture of repentance the gospel calls for in every generation: not defensiveness or hardness when confronted with truth, but immediate, humble sorrow that leads to covenant renewal. This anticipates Christ's own call for all people to repent, and points to the new covenant He establishes, written not merely on stone or scroll but on the hearts of His people, Jeremiah 31:33, Hebrews 8:10, fulfilling what Josiah's public covenant renewal could only symbolize.
basic.god: God's word carries binding authority whether or not it has been read for a generation; its rediscovery does not create new obligations but exposes long neglected ones, provoking exactly the wrath its curses had always warned of. Yet God is also personally attentive to the individual heart within a corporately judged nation, granting Josiah specific, personal mercy because of his tender, humbled response, even while the nation's fixed judgment for its fathers' sins remains unaltered.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 31:24-26 records Moses commanding that the Book of the Law be kept beside the ark, the very scroll apparently lost until Hilkiah's discovery here. 2 Kings 22 gives the parallel account of this same discovery and covenant renewal. Jeremiah 31:33 promises a new covenant written on the heart, the ultimate fulfillment of Josiah's covenant renewal. Isaiah 66:2 commends the one who is humble and contrite in spirit and trembles at God's word, exactly the posture Huldah commends in Josiah.
basic.oneStory: Josiah's story shows that genuine devotion to God can begin before His word is even rediscovered, and that when that word is finally heard, a heart already tender toward God responds immediately with grief, humility, and covenant renewal rather than defensiveness. This pattern of personal mercy granted within a nation's fixed judgment anticipates the new covenant Christ establishes, written on the heart itself, so that every generation, however long God's word has been neglected, can be met with the same tender, immediate response Josiah modeled.

context.literary: The chapter's careful chronology, seeking God at sixteen, purging idolatry at twenty, finding the book at twenty six, deliberately separates personal devotion from the later rediscovery of Scripture, a structure that highlights how thoroughly Josiah's heart was already oriented toward God before the book confirmed and intensified that direction.
context.historical: That the Book of the Law could be entirely lost within the temple itself for likely decades, only to be rediscovered during a repair project, testifies to the severity of the spiritual neglect under Manasseh and Amon's reigns, when Scripture itself, not merely its practice, had apparently been set aside or forgotten.
context.canonical: Josiah's covenant renewal here anticipates and prepares the reader for the fuller account in the following chapters of his restored Passover and eventual tragic death, forming, alongside Hezekiah's earlier reforms, the last great reformation movement before Judah's final decline into exile.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Josiah's personal devotion to God began years before the Book of the Law was rediscovered, and that when it was finally found and read, his already tender heart responded with immediate grief, humble inquiry, and covenant renewal, securing personal mercy for himself even as the nation's judgment for its fathers' sins remained fixed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical rediscovery of a specific physical scroll; it is not suggesting Scripture is normally lost to God's people today or that covenant renewal requires such a dramatic rediscovery. What endures is the pattern of immediate, humble, grief filled response to God's word whenever and however it confronts a hardened generation, and the assurance that personal tenderness of heart matters even within a corporately deserved judgment.

debates.question: How could the Book of the Law have been entirely lost within the temple for a significant period, and what does this suggest about the state of Judah's worship under Manasseh and Amon?

views.label: Deliberate suppression or destruction under Manasseh's severe idolatry
views.case: Given Manasseh's extensive filling of the temple itself with pagan altars and idols, 2 Chronicles 33:4-7, some understand the scroll's disappearance as a direct casualty of that systematic corruption, perhaps deliberately hidden by faithful priests to protect it, or simply buried under decades of institutional neglect and misuse of the temple space.

views.label: Simple, gradual neglect rather than active suppression
views.case: Others read the loss as the natural, if still tragic, consequence of a temple that had not functioned properly for worship in any sustained way for much of two long reigns, so that a physical scroll, without regular liturgical use, was simply misplaced or forgotten among unused chambers during that period.

debates.bearing: Either way, the scroll's rediscovery highlights just how completely God's word had been neglected under the previous two reigns, making Josiah's own prior, independent seeking of God, before the book was ever found, all the more remarkable.

study.originalLanguages: Josiah begins to darash, seek, God as a teenager, well before any physical book confirms his direction. When Hilkiah's discovery is finally read to him, Josiah immediately qara, tears his clothes, the traditional gesture of grief and alarm. Huldah's prophecy singles out his rakak, tender, heart as the reason for his personal mercy, a heart already inclined to soften rather than harden under the weight of God's word.

typology.type: God's long neglected written word, rediscovered and immediately recognized as urgent and authoritative
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of Scripture rediscovered and responded to afresh throughout church history, and ultimately the living Word, Christ Himself, who fulfills all that the written word anticipated, John 1:14.

typology.type: A public covenant renewed and written down, calling the whole people to obedience
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant, written not on stone or scroll but on the human heart itself, Jeremiah 31:33, Hebrews 8:10.

study.christ: Josiah's immediate, tender response to God's rediscovered word, tearing his clothes and humbling himself rather than defending the nation's neglect, models the posture of repentance the gospel calls every generation to when confronted with truth. The covenant Josiah leads the people to renew publicly, written on a scroll and read aloud, anticipates and is surpassed by the new covenant Christ establishes, in which God's law is written directly on the heart, Jeremiah 31:33, Hebrews 8:10, fulfilling and internalizing what Josiah's public renewal could only symbolize outwardly.
study.god: God's word carries binding authority independent of whether it has been read, obeyed, or even remembered by a given generation, and its rediscovery does not create new demands but exposes obligations long neglected. Yet God is intimately attentive to the individual heart even within a corporately deserved judgment, granting Josiah specific, personal mercy for his tender, humbled response even while the fixed consequences of his fathers' generations of sin remain unaltered for the nation as a whole.

crossReferences.note: Moses commands the Book of the Law be kept beside the ark of the covenant, the very scroll apparently lost until Hilkiah's rediscovery here.
The parallel account of this same discovery, Josiah's grief, and Huldah's prophecy.
I will put My law in their minds and write it on their hearts, the new covenant fulfillment of the covenant renewal Josiah leads here.
This is the one I esteem, he who is humble and contrite in spirit and trembles at My word, exactly the posture Huldah commends in Josiah.

study.oneStory: Josiah's personal devotion to God, begun years before any book confirmed it, and his immediate, tender response the moment God's long lost word was finally read to him, together show that genuine faithfulness recognizes and receives God's word rightly rather than needing it to spark a first love for Him. His public covenant renewal, and the personal mercy granted him for his tender heart even amid a fixed national judgment, anticipates the new covenant Christ would establish, writing God's law not on a rediscovered scroll but directly on the hearts of His people.

quiz.q: When did Josiah begin seeking the God of David, relative to the discovery of the Book of the Law?

options.text: He began seeking God at sixteen years old, years before the Book of the Law was found in his eighteenth year of reign.
options.note: Verses 3 and 8 establish this timeline, showing his personal devotion preceded the book's discovery.

options.text: He began seeking God only after the Book of the Law was read to him.
options.note: The text explicitly places his seeking of God years earlier, in the eighth year of his reign, well before the book was found in the eighteenth year.

options.text: He never personally sought God but only followed his officials' instructions.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly and personally attributes this seeking to Josiah himself, not merely to his officials' direction.

options.text: No timeline for Josiah's seeking of God is given.
options.note: Verses 3 and 8 give a specific, datable timeline for these events.

quiz.q: What was found during the temple repairs commissioned by Josiah?

options.text: Hilkiah the priest found the Book of the Law of the LORD given by Moses.
options.note: Verse 14 records this exact discovery.

options.text: A hidden treasury of gold and silver.
options.note: The significant discovery recorded is the Book of the Law itself, not hidden treasure.

options.text: The bones of a former king.
options.note: The discovery is explicitly a written scroll, the Book of the Law, not human remains.

options.text: Nothing significant was found during the repairs.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly records this significant discovery.

quiz.q: How did Josiah respond when he heard the words of the Law read aloud?

options.text: He tore his clothes and sent officials to inquire of the LORD concerning the nation's guilt.
options.note: Verses 19 to 21 record this immediate, grieved response.

options.text: He dismissed the book as irrelevant to his own reign.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a grieved, urgent response, the opposite of dismissal.

options.text: He ordered the book destroyed to avoid its difficult message.
options.note: Josiah instead sought further understanding through Huldah's prophecy, not destruction of the book.

options.text: No reaction from Josiah is recorded.
options.note: Verses 19 to 21 explicitly record his immediate reaction.

quiz.q: What personal assurance did Huldah's prophecy give to Josiah specifically?

options.text: Because his heart was tender and he humbled himself, he would be gathered to his grave in peace and would not see the coming calamity.
options.note: Verses 27 to 28 give this exact, personal assurance to Josiah.

options.text: That the coming judgment on Judah would be entirely canceled.
options.note: The prophecy explicitly states the judgment remains fixed for the nation, while granting personal mercy to Josiah alone regarding when he would see it.

options.text: That Josiah himself would personally witness Jerusalem's destruction.
options.note: The prophecy explicitly promises the opposite, that Josiah's own eyes would not see that calamity.

options.text: No personal message was given to Josiah in the prophecy.
options.note: Verses 26 to 28 give an explicit, personal message directed to Josiah.

quiz.q: What did Josiah do after receiving Huldah's prophecy?

options.text: He gathered all the elders and people, read the Book of the Covenant aloud, and led the whole assembly in publicly renewing the covenant with the LORD.
options.note: Verses 29 to 32 record this public covenant renewal ceremony.

options.text: He kept the discovery of the book private and made no public response.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a large, public assembly and covenant renewal, not private, unshared knowledge.

options.text: He abdicated the throne in despair over the coming judgment.
options.note: Josiah led decisive covenant renewal and continued reforms rather than abdicating in despair.

options.text: No further action from Josiah is recorded after the prophecy.
options.note: Verses 29 to 33 give a detailed account of his subsequent public covenant renewal and continued reforms.

questions: Josiah began seeking God years before he had access to the full written Law. What has shaped your own pursuit of God even in seasons when you have not had full clarity or complete understanding of His word?

When the Law was finally read to him, Josiah's immediate response was grief and humility rather than defensiveness. How do you typically respond when Scripture confronts an area of neglect in your own life or in your community?
Huldah commended Josiah specifically for having a tender heart. Would you describe your own heart's current posture toward correction from God's word as tender or hardened, and why?

Personal mercy was granted to Josiah even though the nation's judgment remained fixed for the sins of prior generations. How do you make sense of experiencing God's specific kindness to you personally even amid painful circumstances you did not cause?

Josiah led the entire nation in publicly renewing their covenant commitment together. What would it look like for you to renew your own commitment to God's word, not privately alone, but together with others who need the same reminder?

translationNotes.note: The Book of the Law given by Moses likely refers to the Pentateuch, or at minimum Deuteronomy, given the covenant language and curses described in the following verses, consistent with the parallel account in 2 Kings 22:8-13.

Huldah the prophetess is one of very few women in the Old Testament explicitly identified by this title; her authoritative prophetic word is sought out directly by the king's own officials without hesitation, underscoring her recognized standing.

You will be gathered to your grave in peace anticipates Josiah's own death, recorded in the following chapter, as occurring before Judah's final destruction by Babylon, sparing him from witnessing that specific calamity, even though his death itself came in battle rather than through natural causes.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 34:1-33. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter.
